description: Quality rules for arcade-style action games — sprite anatomy (ag-016), attack animations (ag-017), keyboard input, rAF loop, collision, off-screen spawn (ag-018). Covers named-game canaries: lane-hopper arcade, maze-chase arcade, flap-and-joust, rescue-shooter, formation shooter, vector-shooter arcade, segmented-bug shooter, cube-hopper, bucket-catch arcade, burger-stomp arcade, gold-grab platformer, top-down road shooter, pseudo-3D racer, skee-ball arcade, Pinball, Tempest, Adventure, Boxing, Snake, Breakout, paddle-bounce, falling-block puzzle.

guidelines.rule: The game must respond to keyboard input immediately. Use keydown events (not keypress) for game controls. Arrow keys, WASD, or Space should work.
guidelines.why: Arcade games need instant responsive controls. Laggy or non-functional controls make the game unplayable.
examples: document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKey)
preventDefault() on arrow keys to stop page scrolling

guidelines.rule: Use requestAnimationFrame for the game loop, NOT setInterval. Target 60fps. Delta-time based movement for consistent speed.
guidelines.why: setInterval causes inconsistent frame rates and janky movement. rAF syncs with the display refresh.
examples: function gameLoop(timestamp) { update(dt); render(); requestAnimationFrame(gameLoop); }

guidelines.rule: Show score, lives, and level prominently. Include a game-over screen with final score and restart option.
guidelines.why: Players need constant feedback on their performance.
examples: HUD at top: Score: 1250 | Lives: 3 | Level: 2
Game Over overlay with 'Press Space to restart'

guidelines.rule: Implement proper collision detection. For rectangles use AABB, for circles use distance checks.
guidelines.why: Bad collision detection — objects passing through each other or false collisions — ruins gameplay.
examples: AABB: if (a.x < b.x+b.w && a.x+a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y+b.h && a.y+a.h > b.y)

guidelines.rule: Snake game MUST grow correctly when eating food, and MUST end when the snake hits a wall or its own body. The snake body segments MUST follow the head exactly — each segment moves to where the previous segment was. Food MUST appear at a random empty cell, never on the snake body.
guidelines.why: Common Snake bugs: snake passes through walls instead of dying, body segments don't follow correctly creating gaps, food spawns on snake body, score doesn't increase on eat.
examples: Grow: on eat, push new tail segment instead of removing old tail
Self-collision: head position matches any body segment = game over
Food spawn: filter out cells occupied by snake body

guidelines.rule: Minesweeper MUST implement: left-click to reveal a cell, right-click to toggle flag, flood-fill auto-reveal for empty cells (cells with 0 adjacent mines), correct mine count in adjacent cells (1-8), first-click safety (never a mine on the first click), win condition (all non-mine cells revealed).
guidelines.why: Minesweeper without flood-fill is essentially unplayable. First-click safety is essential — dying on move 1 is not fun.
examples: Flood-fill: when revealing a 0-cell, recursively reveal all 8 neighbours
First-click: place mines AFTER first click, excluding clicked cell and its neighbours
Flag: right-click toggles 🚩, flagged cells cannot be revealed by left-click

guidelines.rule: four-in-a-row MUST alternate turns between Red and Yellow, check for 4-in-a-row in all 4 directions (horizontal, vertical, diagonal ↗, diagonal ↘) after every move, highlight the winning 4 chips, and prevent moves into full columns.
guidelines.why: Games that don't check diagonals allow undetected wins. Overflowing a full column corrupts board state.
examples: Check all 4 directions from last-placed chip
Highlight winning chips: add CSS class .win to the 4 winning cells
Column full: disable click on that column or show visual indicator

guidelines.rule: Battleship MUST have a 10×10 grid for each player. Ship placement (5 ships: Carrier×5, Battleship×4, Cruiser×3, Submarine×3, Destroyer×2) must validate no overlaps and no out-of-bounds. AI opponent fires intelligently after a hit (target adjacent cells). Show hit/miss on the opponent's board with contrasting colors.
guidelines.why: Battleship without hit-targeting AI is trivially easy. Ship placement validation prevents impossible board states.
examples: After AI hits, push adjacent cells into a target queue before reverting to random
Hit = red, Miss = white dot on dark, Sunk ship reveals all cells of that ship
Show both grids: 'Your Fleet' (ships visible) and 'Enemy Waters' (ships hidden)

guidelines.rule: Simon Says / Memory sequence game MUST show the sequence with clear color flashes (each color lights up with a 400ms on / 200ms off pattern), play a distinct tone per color, accept user input ONLY after the sequence finishes playing, detect wrong input immediately and show game-over, increase sequence by 1 each successful round.
guidelines.why: Simon clones frequently play the sequence and accept input simultaneously, making it impossible to follow. Or they don't detect wrong input until the sequence ends.
examples: Use AudioContext to play: 261Hz (green), 329Hz (red), 392Hz (yellow), 523Hz (blue)
Disable buttons while showing sequence: buttons.forEach(b => b.disabled = true)
Show current round: 'Round 5 — sequence of 5 colors'

guidelines.rule: Arcade games MUST save high scores to localStorage AND submit to the global leaderboard on game-over using `window.mChatAI?.leaderboard?.submit({ score: finalScore, metadata: { level, lives_lost } })`. Show the user's global rank on the game-over screen. Render a top-10 leaderboard table with names and scores.
guidelines.why: Local high scores vanish on reinstall and provide no competitive incentive. Leaderboard turns 'I beat my score' into 'I beat everyone'. Zero-config with the mChatAI bridge — 3 lines of code.
examples: Game over: leaderboard.submit({ score, metadata: { level: currentLevel, waveReached: wave } }).then(r => showGameOver(r.rank, r.percentile));
Top-10 table: leaderboard.getTop(10).then(scores => { scores.forEach((s,i) => addRow(i+1, s.displayName, s.score)); });
localStorage fallback: always save to localStorage too, show local best even without account

guidelines.rule: Atari/retro-style games MUST use a pixel-art aesthetic: chunky pixels rendered on a canvas using integer coordinates, a limited palette of 4-16 bold colors (no gradients or anti-aliasing), blocky sans-serif font (monospace or bitmap-style), and scanline or CRT overlay (optional but strongly recommended). The canvas should fill the viewport with `imageSmoothingEnabled = false`.
guidelines.why: Atari-style games that use modern smooth CSS gradients, rounded corners, or system fonts break the retro illusion completely. The pixelated aesthetic IS the product — without it the game feels like a bad clone rather than a loving tribute.
examples: ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false; — must be set on every canvas resize
Canvas scale: draw at 320×240 internal resolution, CSS-scale up to fill the screen
Palette: use 2600-style colors — black #000, white #FFF, orange #F77F00, cyan #00FFFF, green #00FF00, red #FF0000, purple #7700FF
Font: use 'Courier New' or embed a pixel font via @font-face
Scanlines: draw semi-transparent horizontal lines every 2px over the canvas for CRT effect
Sound: use AudioContext square-wave oscillators (type:'square') for authentic beeps, not sine waves

guidelines.rule: Atari-style wave-shooter arcade / formation shooter MUST: render the player ship as a pixel-art sprite at the bottom, alien grid of 5×11 marching left/right and descending, aliens shoot back at increasing rate as their count decreases, bunkers erode pixel-by-pixel when hit, UFO bonus target crosses top. Player has 3 lives shown as mini ship sprites.
guidelines.why: wave-shooter arcade clones without alien return fire, descending movement, or bunker erosion miss the core tension. The game gets harder as you kill aliens — fewer aliens move faster — this is the key difficulty curve.
examples: Alien march speed: baseSpeed * (totalAliens / startingAliens) — gets faster as aliens die
Alien descent: move down by alienHeight when hitting a wall
Bunkers: use a pixel buffer (ImageData) that removes individual pixels on bullet hit
3 alien types: top row = 30pts, middle rows = 20pts, bottom rows = 10pts
UFO: appears at top every 25-30 seconds, worth 50-300 random points

guidelines.rule: Atari-style vector-shooter arcade MUST: render vector-style ship (triangle outline), implement screen wrapping (objects exit one side and enter the other), ship thrust with momentum/inertia (no instant stop), asteroids split into 2 smaller ones when shot (large→medium→small→destroyed), ship is invincible for 3 seconds after respawn, hyperspace (random teleport) as emergency escape.
guidelines.why: vector-shooter arcade without screen-wrapping and inertia feels completely wrong — these are the two defining physics properties of the game. vector-shooter arcade that disappear instead of splitting remove the core difficulty escalation.
examples: Wrapping: if (x < 0) x = canvas.width; if (x > canvas.width) x = 0; (same for y)
Inertia: vx += Math.cos(angle) * thrust * dt; x += vx * dt; vx *= 0.99; (friction)
Split: large asteroid (r=40) → 2 medium (r=20); medium → 2 small (r=10); small → destroyed
Respawn: new ship at center, 3s invincibility shown by blinking

guidelines.rule: top-down adventure-style games MUST: implement a multi-room dungeon map (at least 20 rooms connected by doors), a player avatar (blocky square or arrow), items the player can pick up and carry (sword, key, bridge), enemies that chase the player with simple pathfinding, and a win condition (reach the chalice). Rooms must persist state (items stay where dropped). Include a map legend or mini-map.
guidelines.why: Adventure (2600) is the ancestor of all action-adventure games. Its defining features — inventory, multi-room exploration, key-locked doors — must all be present. Without them it's just a maze game.
examples: Room connections: roomData[id].exits = { north: 5, south: 2, east: null, west: 8 }
Inventory: player can carry 1 item; picking up a new item drops the current one
Dragons: patrol a room, reverse direction at walls, chase player when visible
Sword: kills dragons on contact when player is holding it
Keys: unlock castle doors (colored door requires matching colored key)
Bridge: allows crossing walls (place it against a wall to pass through)

guidelines.rule: Wave-based / level-based shooters MUST implement a level-clear → next-wave transition. When all enemies on the current wave are destroyed, IMMEDIATELY advance to the next wave: respawn enemies (with increased difficulty: more enemies, faster spawn, or new behavior), bump a wave counter visible in the HUD, and reset the player's position if appropriate. The game cannot be 'won' on a single wave alone — there must be a sense of progression.
guidelines.why: formation shooter + segmented-bug shooter first-pass canaries 2026-04-25 both shipped with terminal-level-1 bugs: clearing all enemies on level 1 left an empty playfield with no progression. Players had nothing to do. The level-clear → next-wave loop is a defining property of arcade shooters; without it, they're not games, they're tech demos. Easy to overlook because LLMs render the FIRST wave and assume the loop is implicit.
examples: Wave transition: function checkWaveClear() { if (enemies.length === 0 && bullets.length === 0) startNextWave(); }
Wave scaling: function startNextWave() { wave++; const enemyCount = 10 + wave * 2; const enemySpeed = baseSpeed * (1 + wave * 0.15); spawnEnemies(enemyCount, enemySpeed); }

HUD wave counter:
Wave {wave}
updated every frame
Brief pause between waves: setTimeout(startNextWave, 1500) so player can breathe
Boss wave optional: every 5 waves, spawn 1 large boss with HP > 1
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-25 first-pass beta of galaga-079 + centipede-080 — both stuck on level 1

guidelines.rule: When the goal NAMES a recognizable arcade game by title (Boxing, lane-hopper arcade, maze-chase arcade, flap-and-joust, rescue-shooter, pseudo-3D racer, cube-hopper, bucket-catch arcade, burger-stomp arcade, gold-grab platformer, top-down road shooter, skee-ball arcade, Pinball, formation shooter, segmented-bug shooter, Tempest, etc.), the player avatar and primary game-objects MUST be MULTI-PART COMPOSITE SPRITES that visually match that game's iconic silhouette — NOT solid-color rectangles or circles. Minimum bar: ≥4 visually distinct parts (head + body + 2 limbs for figures; cab + wheels + windshield for vehicles; ladders + platforms + chef + components for burger-stomp arcade). A 'red square vs green square' fails this rule for every named game in this list.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-04-27: Boxing canary 140 first-pass shipped two solid-fill rectangles (red, green) standing in for the boxers. The original 2600 Boxing rendered each boxer as a stick figure with HEAD + BODY + LEFT-ARM + RIGHT-ARM, where each ARM extends and retracts to throw a punch (the punch animation IS the gameplay readout — you can see who's exposed). Without recognizable anatomy, the gameplay loop is unreadable; without the extending-arm visual, 'punch' has no visual signal. Same pattern threatens any named-game canary. This rule forces the planner/generator to plan the sprite anatomy as a deliberate spec line, not an afterthought. (Second-turn auto-iteration on the same canary produced proper stick-figure boxers — proving the wizard CAN do it when the rule is pulled into context; this rule makes that the default, not the recovery.)
examples: // CORRECT: Boxing sprite anatomy
// player parts: { head: circle r=8 at (cx, cy-22), body: rect 6x16 at (cx-3, cy-14),
//                 leftArm: rect 4x10 at (cx-7, cy-10) extends to (cx-15, cy-10) on left-jab,
//                 rightArm: rect 4x10 at (cx+3, cy-10) extends to (cx+11, cy-10) on right-jab }
// punch animation = arm rect extends 8px outward over 100ms then retracts
// CORRECT: lane-hopper arcade frog anatomy
// { body: rounded-rect green 16x12, head: circle 8x8 forward, eyes: 2 black 2x2 dots,
//   legs: 4 small triangles at corners, animated to alternate fold/extend on hop }
// CORRECT: maze-chase arcade
// { body: yellow circle r=12 with mouth = pie-slice cut from -45° to +45°, animated open/close at 8Hz }
// BAD
// const player = { x, y, w: 30, h: 30, color: 'green' };  ctx.fillRect(...)
// (a green rectangle is not lane-hopper arcade. A red rectangle is not a boxer.)
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-27 boxing-140 canary — turn-1 was two solid-fill squares with no anatomy; turn-2 self-corrected to stick figures (proving capability), but the default should be anatomy-from-the-start

guidelines.rule: Boxing / fighting / 1v1-melee games MUST show ATTACK ANIMATIONS that visibly extend the attacking limb away from the body (left jab = left arm extends 8-12px outward over 80-120ms then retracts; power punch = arm extends further with telegraph wind-up frames). The opponent's hit-reaction MUST be visible (head flinches back 4-6px, body flashes white for 100ms, OR a 'POW!' text burst appears). Without visible attack + reaction, players can't tell if a punch landed and fights become guess-and-check.
guidelines.why: Boxing-140 canary 2026-04-27 first-pass had 'punch' as a state-toggle with score ticker but no visible attack animation — the player saw their score tick up but couldn't see the punch happen. Same gap threatens every fighting/melee entry. Genre convention from Boxing → Punch-Out → every modern fighter: arm extends OUT to throw, opponent flinches BACK on hit, score updates AFTER the visible exchange. The visual sequence IS the gameplay — without it, players can't read the fight.
examples: // CORRECT: jab animation (60fps, ~6 frames = 100ms)
function startJab(boxer, dir) {  // dir = 1 (right) or -1 (left)
boxer.armExtension = 0;
const tween = setInterval(() => {
boxer.armExtension += 2 * dir;  // extend 2px per frame
if (Math.abs(boxer.armExtension) >= 10) {
clearInterval(tween);
// hit-detection at peak extension
if (overlap(boxer.fistBox, opponent.bodyBox)) registerHit(opponent);
retract(boxer);  // separate tween bringing arm back
// CORRECT: hit reaction
function registerHit(target) {
target.flashWhite = true; setTimeout(() => target.flashWhite = false, 100);
target.headOffset.x -= 5;  // flinch back
setTimeout(() => target.headOffset.x = 0, 150);
target.score = Math.max(0, target.score - 1);
spawnPow(target.x, target.y - 20);  // 'POW!' text burst
// BAD: invisible punch — score ticks but nothing visible happens
// if (button === 'punch') { opponent.score--; }
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-27 boxing-140 — punch was state-toggle with score change, no visible attack on first pass

guidelines.rule: Side-scrolling and lane-traffic spawners (cars in lane-hopper arcade, ghosts in maze-chase arcade, fish in Fishing Derby, vehicles in top-down road shooter, asteroids in rescue-shooter, falling bombs in bucket-catch arcade, missiles in Missile Command) MUST spawn entities OFF-SCREEN, then scroll them ON-SCREEN at their lane velocity. Spawning AT random visible positions (e.g. `entity.x = Math.random() * canvas.width`) causes cars/logs/enemies to BLINK INTO EXISTENCE in front of the player — unfair, unreadable, and breaks genre convention. Standard pattern: spawn at `x = (lane.direction > 0) ? -entity.width : canvas.width`; despawn at the opposite edge with `+ entity.width` margin so they fully exit before recycling.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-04-27: lane-hopper arcade-pond canary first-pass had cars + logs popping into existence at random visible X positions instead of scrolling on from off-screen. Player reported 'I just see a car pop up' — fair-play impossible because entities materialize without warning. Same anti-pattern threatens every lane-traffic / scrolling-spawner game. Genre convention from the original lane-hopper arcade (1981), maze-chase arcade (1980), and every modern endless-runner: visible entities ALWAYS originate from beyond the screen edge in their lane's direction. The bug is easy to write because `Math.random() * canvas.width` is the obvious-looking 'random spawn' code; the test is whether the LLM remembers the off-screen anchor.
examples: // CORRECT: lane-aware off-screen spawn
function spawnCar(lane) {
const dir = lane.direction;  // +1 = moves right, -1 = moves left
const x = dir > 0 ? -CAR_W : canvas.width;  // start fully off-screen on the entry edge
cars.push({ x, y: lane.y, vx: dir * lane.speed, w: CAR_W, h: CAR_H, lane });
// CORRECT: despawn after car has fully exited the opposite edge
function tickCar(c, dt) {
c.x += c.vx * dt;
if (c.vx > 0 && c.x > canvas.width + 4) return false;  // off right edge → recycle
if (c.vx < 0 && c.x < -c.w - 4) return false;          // off left edge → recycle
return true;
// BAD: random visible spawn — entities pop into existence in front of the player
// const x = Math.random() * canvas.width;  // ← causes blink-in
// CORRECT: spawn cadence (Poisson-like) decoupled from spawn position
if (lane.spawnTimer <= 0) { spawnCar(lane); lane.spawnTimer = lane.spawnInterval + (Math.random() - 0.5) * lane.spawnJitter; }
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-27 frogger-132 — cars + logs spawned at random visible X instead of off-screen; player can't react

guidelines.title: Forced-perspective sports games: screen Y is DEPTH, not HEIGHT — no gravity along screen-Y
guidelines.rule: When a game shows a forced-perspective top-down lane (skee-ball arcade, bowling, shuffleboard, curling, basketball-at-hoop, mini-golf-putt, billiards-cue-end, beer-pong, ring-toss, darts-from-line), the screen Y-axis represents WORLD DEPTH (distance into the lane), NOT vertical height. The ball/disc rolls horizontally on a flat plane and gravity acts perpendicular to the screen — which means **gravity does NOT pull the ball back toward the player along screen-Y**. The ONLY deceleration is rolling friction. NEVER write `vy += GRAVITY` against screen-Y in this class of game.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-04-28 skee-ball arcade canary: LLM modeled the lane as a 2D platformer, set `GRAVITY=0.14` on screen-vy, and wrote `ball.vy += GRAVITY`. Result: when the player flicks UP (vy negative, ball moves toward target rings at top of screen), gravity decelerates the ball, brings it to rest, then accelerates it BACK toward the player. The ball never reaches the rings no matter how hard the player flicks — it 'rises and falls' as if thrown straight up under gravity. This is a category error: in a forced-perspective top-down view, screen-Y is a projection of world-depth (Z in 3D), not world-Y (height). World gravity points along world-Y which is OUT OF the screen, so it has zero component along screen-Y. The bug is easy to write because 'ball + arc + gravity' is the LLM's strongest physics pattern and forced-perspective is rarer training data. The fix is structural: model the ball as moving on a 2D top-down plane where vy is depth-velocity, vx is lateral-velocity, and the only deceleration is friction (vy *= friction; vx *= friction).
examples: // BAD: 2D-platformer physics applied to a forced-perspective top-down lane
// var GRAVITY = 0.14;
// ball.vy += GRAVITY;  // ← pulls ball back toward player; ball NEVER reaches target
// ball.x += ball.vx; ball.y += ball.vy;
// CORRECT: top-down rolling physics — gravity is perpendicular to screen, only friction decelerates
var ROLL_FRICTION = 0.985;  // tune so a strong flick reaches the far end in ~1.2-1.8s
function stepBall(ball) {
ball.vx *= ROLL_FRICTION;
ball.vy *= ROLL_FRICTION;  // depth-velocity decays from rolling friction ONLY
ball.x += ball.vx;
ball.y += ball.vy;       // NOTE: no `ball.vy += GRAVITY` — there is no gravity along screen-Y
if (Math.hypot(ball.vx, ball.vy) < 0.3) settleAndScore(ball);
// If you want the ball to APPEAR to rise off the lane into the target ring (like real skee-ball arcade's
// lip), use a SEPARATE perceived-Z that fakes the arc visually but doesn't affect lane physics:
// ballScreenY = ballLaneY - perceivedHeight(ballLaneY);  // perceivedHeight() arcs only near the lip
// Lane physics still operates on (vx, vy) with friction-only decel.
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-28 skee-ball-152 — LLM applied 2D-platformer gravity to screen-vy; ball flicked UP rises, slows, falls back to start. User: 'instead of going INTO the screen and landing in the holes, ball rises up vertically and then falls back to the ground back to the bottom of the screen'.

guidelines.title: Forced-perspective targets: hit-test in WORLD coordinates, not screen pixels
guidelines.rule: When the target (skee-ball arcade rings, dart board, basketball hoop, bowling pins, curling button) is drawn at the perspective-far end of the lane, the visible pixel size is SMALLER than the world hit-radius because of foreshortening. Hit-test against the WORLD radius at the ball's depth, not against the rendered ring's screen radius. Rule of thumb: at lane-far (small screen-y), the world target radius corresponds to a LARGER hit-tolerance in pixels than the rendered ring suggests; at lane-near it's smaller. Without this, players who 'visually' land in the 100-ring score 0 because their pixel-distance to the ring center is larger than the rendered-ring radius.
guidelines.why: Companion rule to ag-019. After fixing the gravity model, the next pitfall is scoring: the rendered rings are drawn small (perspective-far), but the actual tolerance for 'this ball hit the 100' should be the ball's lane footprint vs. the world ring. Naive `dist = hypot(ball.x - ringCx, ball.y - ringCy); if (dist < ring.r) score = ring.pts;` works when ring.r is in world units but fails when ring.r is the small RENDERED radius. Either render rings at world-size (bigger pixels at the lane-far end, which looks weird in forced-perspective) OR keep two radii: `ring.renderR` (for drawing) and `ring.worldR` (for hit-test, larger).
examples: // CORRECT: separate render-radius from hit-radius
var RINGS = [
{ worldR: 22, renderR: 18, pts: 100 },  // hit-test forgiving; render small for perspective
{ worldR: 38, renderR: 32, pts: 50 },
{ worldR: 56, renderR: 48, pts: 30 },
{ worldR: 78, renderR: 68, pts: 20 },
{ worldR: 100, renderR: 88, pts: 10 }
function score(ball) {
var d = Math.hypot(ball.x - RING_CX, ball.y - RING_CY);
for (var r of RINGS) if (d <= r.worldR) return r.pts;  // hit-test on worldR
return 0;
// Rendering uses renderR for the visible perspective look:
// ctx.arc(RING_CX, RING_CY, ring.renderR, 0, Math.PI * 2);
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-28 skee-ball-152 followup — even after fixing gravity, naive radius hit-test against perspective-shrunk render-radius produces 'visually in the ring but scored 0' frustration.

guidelines.rule: SNAKE GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions snake, classic snake, mobile snake, snake clone
guidelines.guidance:
When generating ANY snake-style game, your output MUST satisfy ALL these invariants — verify each before declaring complete:

1. **Snake cannot reverse onto itself.** When user presses opposite direction (e.g. Right while moving Left), reject the input OR queue it for the next tick where it would be a 90° turn — never apply on the current tick.
2. **Eating an apple grows the snake by EXACTLY +1 segment** at the tail position, and a NEW apple appears at a random empty cell (not on the snake body).
3. **Game over conditions** (both must be checked every tick): snake head hits a wall (or wraps if the goal explicitly says wrap), AND snake head touches any of its own body segments.
4. **Score = (segments - initialLength) × 10**. Persist best score in localStorage with key 'snakeBestScore' or similar.
5. **Speed**: tick interval starts ~150ms, decreases 2-5% per apple eaten (cap at minimum ~50ms). Use setInterval OR requestAnimationFrame with delta-time.
6. **Input buffering**: if user presses two keys in quick succession, queue the second so it applies after the first. Without this, fast players feel input-loss.
7. **Initial state**: snake is 3 segments at center, moving right at 1 cell/tick.
8. **Mobile**: support both arrow keys AND swipe gestures (touchstart/touchmove/touchend with 30px+ threshold).
guidelines.example: Lawrence-flagged 2026-04-30: original snake had no keyboard handlers — autofix had to add them after Phase 0d.27 caught it. With this checklist injected, the first generation should wire keydown listeners up front.
guidelines.learnedFrom: 2026-04-30 snake canaries v1-v8: model produced 2D grid + draw loop but consistently missed (a) keyboard handlers, (b) reverse-prevention, (c) speed acceleration. With this consolidated invariant block, all three should land in the first turn.

guidelines.rule: ASTEROIDS GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions asteroids, space shooter, rotate ship, vector arcade
guidelines.guidance:
When generating ANY vector-shooter arcade-style space shooter, satisfy ALL these:

1. **Ship physics**: ship rotates by Left/Right (constant angular velocity ~0.05 rad/frame), thrust by Up (apply velocity in ship's facing direction at constant force).
2. **Velocity friction**: multiply velocity by ~0.98 each frame so the ship coasts gradually after thrust released, not infinitely.
3. **Wrap-around**: when ship/bullet/asteroid exits screen edge, wrap to opposite edge (NOT bounce). Without this, asteroids escape and player has nothing to shoot.
4. **Shoot**: Space fires a bullet from ship's nose, traveling in ship's current angle at high speed. Bullets despawn after ~1 second OR on screen exit.
5. **Asteroid splitting**: when shot, large asteroids split into 2 medium; medium split into 2 small; small disappear. Total 3 size tiers.
6. **Asteroid spawning**: 4-6 large asteroids at start. New wave when all destroyed (level up).
7. **Game over**: ship collides with asteroid → lose life. 3 lives total. After last life, show score + Game Over screen.
8. **Score**: large=20, medium=50, small=100. Persist high score.
9. **Visuals**: classic style is vector outline (stroke, no fill) on black background, white/cyan stroke. Use `ctx.stroke()` not `ctx.fill()` for the ship/asteroid shapes.
guidelines.learnedFrom: 2026-04-30 asteroid-snake mashup canary: produced 18KB working code in 1 turn but rotation+thrust physics was correct only because the goal explicitly described it. Without this checklist, snake-genre prompt would not have produced asteroids physics — fragile.

guidelines.rule: PAC-MAN GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions pacman, pac-man, maze game, ghosts and dots
guidelines.guidance:
maze-chase arcade-style mazes have 8+ specific rules. Satisfy ALL:

1. **Grid-based movement**: player and ghosts move tile-by-tile at constant speed (don't free-fly). Tile size ~20-30px.
2. **4-direction input + buffering**: user can queue NEXT direction by pressing it while moving — it applies at the next intersection where that direction is valid (a wall isn't blocking).
3. **Maze walls block movement**: collision check before each tile move; if next tile is wall, halt motion, retain queued direction.
4. **Dots**: every non-wall tile has a dot. Player overlapping dot → +10 points, remove dot.
5. **Power-pellets**: 4 large dots in corners. Eating one → ghosts enter 'frightened' mode for ~8 seconds (visual: ghosts turn blue/dark). During frightened, collision with ghost = ghost respawns at center + +200 points.
6. **Ghost AI**: 4 ghosts with distinct personalities is the hallmark — at minimum implement TWO of: red enemy (chase nearest tile to player), pink enemy (target 4 tiles ahead of player's facing), orange enemy (random walk), Inky (alternates chase + scatter).
7. **Lives**: 3 lives. Non-frightened ghost collision = lose life + reset positions.
8. **Win condition**: all dots eaten → level complete. Optionally next level with faster ghosts.
9. **Tunnel wrap**: most maze-chase arcade boards have a horizontal tunnel at the middle row that wraps left ↔ right edges.
guidelines.learnedFrom: 2026-04-30 pacman canary: model produced ASTEROIDS-style ship rotation code (ship.angle, ship.thrust, ArrowLeft/Right rotate) for a pacman maze goal — completely wrong genre. Lawrence flagged in screenshot. maze-chase arcade is grid + maze + dots, not free-flying ship. This checklist forces the right mental model upfront.

guidelines.rule: FROGGER GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions frogger, dodge cars, cross road, lily pad
guidelines.guidance:
lane-hopper arcade has 3 distinct play zones top-to-bottom. Satisfy ALL:

1. **Three zones top-to-bottom**: 5 lily pads (target/safe), river (with logs+turtles, deadly water), grass median (safe), road (with cars, deadly), starting bottom (safe).
2. **Cars on the road**: 4-5 lanes, each with cars moving horizontally at different speeds + directions. Sprite + speed varies per lane.
3. **Logs and turtles on the river**: player MUST stand on a log/turtle to cross. Floating moves player along with the log. Stepping into water (gap between logs) = lose life.
4. **Turtle dive**: turtles periodically submerge for ~2 seconds. Player on a submerged turtle = lose life. Adds tension vs. logs (always-safe).
5. **Goal**: reach all 5 lily pads. Each lily pad reached = score + reset to start.
6. **Time limit**: ~30-60 seconds per crossing. Time runs out = lose life.
7. **Lives**: 3 lives. Game over when 0.
8. **Movement**: arrow keys move ONE tile per press (not continuous). Each move bumps player up/down/left/right by exactly 1 cell. Don't free-scroll.
9. **Score**: per-row points crossing forward + bonus for reaching lily pad.
guidelines.learnedFrom: Common frogger canary failure: model omits the river/log mechanic entirely, just makes a road-crossing game. River is the harder half and the genre-defining feature.

guidelines.rule: TETRIS GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions tetris, falling blocks, 4-cell piece, line clear
guidelines.guidance:
falling-block puzzle is a falling-block puzzle. Satisfy ALL:

1. **7 4-cell piece shapes**: I (long), O (square), T, S, Z, L, J. Define each as a 4×4 grid of cells. Random selection (or 7-bag for fairness).
2. **Playfield**: 10 wide × 20 tall. Visible top is 20 (older Tetrises hide top 4 rows for spawn).
3. **Piece falls continuously**: every X ms (start ~1000, accelerate per level), the active piece moves down 1 row.
4. **Controls**: Left/Right move horizontally (1 cell). Up = rotate clockwise. Down = soft drop (faster fall). Space = hard drop (slam to bottom + lock).
5. **Wall kicks**: when rotating near walls, try simple offsets (-1, +1, -2, +2) to find a valid placement before giving up.
6. **Lock + line clear**: when piece can't fall further (collides with floor or another piece), lock its cells into the playfield. Then check each row top-to-bottom; FULL rows clear (all cells filled). Award points: 1 line=100, 2=300, 3=500, 4=800 (falling-block puzzle bonus).
7. **Level + speed**: every 10 lines cleared, level++ and fall speed × 0.85 (accelerates).
8. **Game over**: new piece spawns and immediately collides → game over.
9. **Next-piece preview**: show the next 1-3 pieces in a sidebar.
10. **Hold piece** (optional but classic): C/Shift swaps the active piece with a held piece (only swap once per piece).
guidelines.learnedFrom: Common tetris failure: model produces falling pieces but skips line-clear OR rotates incorrectly OR doesn't accelerate. The 4-line falling-block puzzle bonus (800pts) is also commonly missed.

guidelines.rule: BREAKOUT/ARKANOID GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions breakout, arkanoid, brick breaker, paddle ball
guidelines.guidance:
Breakout is paddle + ball + bricks. Satisfy ALL:

1. **Layout**: bricks at top in a grid (8-12 wide × 4-8 high), paddle at bottom (movable horizontally), ball starts on paddle.
2. **Ball physics**: constant speed, bounces off walls (top/left/right) AND paddle AND bricks. Bouncing reverses the relevant velocity component.
3. **Paddle bounce angle**: when ball hits paddle, the rebound angle depends on WHERE on the paddle it hit. Center → straight up; edges → 45°+. Without this, the game has no skill ceiling.
4. **Brick break**: ball touches brick → remove brick + reverse ball Y-velocity + +N points (varies by row color).
5. **Lives**: 3 lives. Ball below paddle = lose life + reset ball on paddle.
6. **Win**: all bricks cleared = level complete. Optionally next level with faster ball OR more bricks.
7. **Visuals**: bricks colored by row. Classic palette: red/orange/yellow/green top-to-bottom.
8. **Mobile**: on touch, paddle follows finger X-position.
9. **Score persistence**: localStorage best score.
guidelines.learnedFrom: Common breakout failure: ball bounces off paddle straight without angle variation → game becomes deterministic + boring.

guidelines.rule: PONG GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions pong, paddle game, two-paddle, ball game
guidelines.guidance:
paddle-bounce is the simplest genre but commonly butchered. Satisfy ALL:

1. **2 paddles, 1 ball**: left and right paddles, ball in middle.
2. **Paddle motion**: each paddle moves vertically only. Player 1 keys (W/S), Player 2 (Up/Down) OR AI for one side.
3. **Ball physics**: constant speed, bounces off top/bottom walls + paddles. Side walls (left/right) = score for opposite side.
4. **Paddle bounce angle** (same as breakout): rebound angle based on hit location on paddle.
5. **Score**: first to N points (configurable, default 11). Ball resets to center after each score, served toward losing side.
6. **AI opponent**: For Lego artifacts, mount `entities/PongAI` with `delay: 110` (ms) and `error: 0.12` (fraction of paddle height) — the defaults. Call `ai.update(dt)` in your game loop. For non-Lego artifacts, build the equivalent: track ball Y with a 80-150ms reaction delay AND a per-decision random error of ±5-15% of paddle height. WITHOUT delay+error, an AI that simply tracks ball Y is unbeatable, which is a #1 reported paddle-bounce bug.
7. **Visuals**: classic black background, white paddles + ball, dashed center line, large score numbers at top.
8. **Sound**: short beep on paddle hit, different beep on score. Use Web Audio API tones (no sample files).
guidelines.learnedFrom: paddle-bounce canary 2026-04-30: model produced functional pong but AI was 100% accurate (impossible to beat) AND no paddle-position-based bounce angle. Both common. 2026-05-02: PongAI component shipped to enforce delay+error defaults for Lego artifacts.

guidelines.rule: TIC-TAC-TOE GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions tic tac toe, tic-tac-toe, noughts and crosses, X and O, 3x3 grid
guidelines.guidance:
Tic-tac-toe is small but the AI is the hard part. Satisfy ALL:

1. **3×3 grid**: 9 cells, each empty/X/O. Click empty cell to place mark.
2. **Turn alternation**: track whose turn it is, alternate after each valid move.
3. **Win detection**: 8 winning lines (3 rows + 3 cols + 2 diagonals). After every move, check if current player just completed any line.
4. **Draw detection**: all 9 cells filled and no winner = draw.
5. **AI opponent**: implement minimax (perfect play) — AI never loses, only wins or draws. Recursion depth ≤9 so it's fast. Without minimax, AI is too easy.
6. **AI difficulty toggle** (nice-to-have): Easy = random move, Medium = block opponent's threats, Hard = minimax.
7. **Reset**: after win/draw, show 'Play again' button that clears the board.
8. **Visuals**: clear X and O markers, highlight the winning line when won.
9. **Score tracker**: optionally count X-wins / O-wins / Draws across games.
guidelines.learnedFrom: 2026-04-30 tic-tac-toe canary: model produced functional 3x3 grid but AI was random — easy to win, no challenge. Players quit immediately.

guidelines.rule: TOWER DEFENSE GENRE INVARIANTS (must-have checklist)
guidelines.applies_when: Goal mentions tower defense, td game, wave defense, place towers
guidelines.guidance:
Tower defense has many moving parts. Satisfy ALL:

1. **Map with path**: a fixed enemy path from spawn to end. Drawn as a series of waypoints. Enemies follow this path.
2. **Tower placement**: click empty grid cell (not on path) → place tower (after paying gold cost). Towers cannot be placed on the path itself.
3. **Tower types**: at minimum 2 (e.g., basic-fast vs slow-AOE). Each has range, damage, fire-rate, cost.
4. **Targeting**: each frame, each tower picks an enemy in range (closest, OR most-progressed) and fires a projectile toward it. Projectile homes (or is hitscan) and damages on impact.
5. **Enemy waves**: enemies spawn in waves of ~10-20. Pause between waves so player can place towers. After all waves cleared = win.
6. **Enemy types**: vary HP and speed. Fast enemy = low HP, slow tank = high HP.
7. **Gold economy**: kill enemy → gain gold. Place tower → spend gold. Player must balance offense vs. resources.
8. **Lives**: enemies that reach the path's end → -1 life. 0 lives = game over.
9. **Tower upgrade**: clicking placed tower → upgrade button (spend gold for higher damage/range).
guidelines.learnedFrom: TD canary failures: model produces grid + enemies but skips waves (continuous enemy spawn) OR towers don't target (just placeholders) OR no gold economy.

guidelines.rule: Every game MUST present a VISIBLE on-screen restart/play-again button when the game ends (game-over, you-win, level-complete). Do NOT rely solely on a menu shortcut like Cmd+N or 'Game > New Game' — users don't discover those mid-play. The restart UI must: (a) appear automatically when the terminal phase is reached, (b) be visually distinct (colored button or large tappable area), (c) clearly say 'Restart' / 'Play Again' / 'New Game' (not just 'OK' or unlabeled), (d) when tapped, fully reset all game state (score, lives, level, score-multiplier, fuel, terrain — anything that accumulated during play). Keep the menu-bar shortcut too (e.g. Cmd+N), but the on-screen button is the primary affordance.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on shipped thrust-and-land: 'There is no restart button for the game: That should be a fundamental wisdom rule to all games if we don't have that already.' The shipped thrust-and-land rendered 'GAME OVER score=0' as static HUD text with no UI affordance. Users had to discover Cmd+N from the menu bar. Same pattern was hit by earlier shipped paddle-bounce, burger-stomp arcade, flap-and-joust — they all have the menu shortcut but lack a tappable on-screen restart. This is the single most common 'it's a great game but UX feels rough' complaint.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI overlay): When phase becomes .gameOver, render `VStack { Text('GAME OVER').font(.largeTitle); Text('Score: \(score)'); Button('Play Again') { restart() }.buttonStyle(.borderedProminent) }.padding(20).background(...).overlay(...)`
RIGHT (SpriteKit overlay): On game-over, add an SKShapeNode panel with 'Game Over' label + an SKLabelNode 'Tap to Restart' that the scene's touchesBegan/mouseDown handler responds to.
RIGHT (HTML game): On game-over, show a centered modal with 'Play Again' button. The modal is dismissable only by clicking the button (don't auto-dismiss).
WRONG: Showing 'GAME OVER score=0' in the HUD label and stopping the game loop. User can't restart without quitting and relaunching.
WRONG: Only Cmd+N / menu shortcut. Users don't think to look there mid-play.
Bonus: keyboard shortcut on the button itself (e.g. Space or Return = Play Again) for users who prefer keyboard.

guidelines.rule: The 'visible on-screen restart button' rule (mac-022 / ag-restart-button) applies to EVERY terminal phase, not just the obvious 'lose' state. Specifically: (a) win — show 'Play Again' overlay (b) lose — show 'Play Again' overlay (c) draw / stalemate (in 2-player games) — show 'Rematch' overlay (d) puzzle-solved — show 'Next puzzle' or 'Play Again' overlay. If the engine has any phase OTHER than the live 'playing' state, that phase needs a restart UI affordance, not just a status label. Keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+N, R) are an escape hatch but the on-screen button is mandatory.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Minesweeper reference: "A restart button would be nice (instead of just hitting R)". The reference had a 'New Game' button hidden in a controls row + an 'R' keyboard shortcut, but no overlay on the win/lose screen. Same pattern slipped through on the original mac-022 review. Extending the rule explicitly so future reviewers know: every non-playing phase needs an overlay button.
examples: RIGHT: `if engine.state == .won || engine.state == .lost { gameOverOverlay }` with the overlay containing a Button('Play Again') keyboardShortcut(.return) and a status message.
RIGHT (puzzle): when isSolved becomes true, overlay 'New Puzzle' button. NOT 'press Cmd+N' — the button is the affordance.
WRONG: only setting `statusMessage = 'GAME OVER'` and relying on the user to find the small 'New Game' button in a controls row.
Real incident 2026-05-04: shipped thrust-and-land v1 had 'GAME OVER score=0' as static HUD. Catalog Minesweeper reference had hidden 'New Game' in controls. Same family of misses.

guidelines.rule: Game backgrounds MUST be warm/atmospheric - radial-gradient or layered linear-gradient with at least one color highlight per corner. NOT pure black, NOT plain solid color. The web Candy Match reference uses a layered gradient: base purple (#1f1026) + radial yellow glow at top-left + radial cyan glow at top-right. Same pattern works for any game: pick a primary color matching the game's theme (purple for candy, dark blue for space, dark green for forest), add 1-2 radial corner glows in complementary colors. CSS: set the app root to `background: radial-gradient(circle at top left, rgba(255,220,90,0.35), transparent 45%), radial-gradient(circle at top right, rgba(90,220,255,0.30), transparent 45%), #1f1026;` and make it fill the viewport (min-height: 100vh). The warmth makes the game feel like a finished product instead of a wireframe.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The black background is lame'. Switched to layered radial-gradient (purple base + yellow + cyan corners) per the web Candy Match reference. The atmosphere is the difference between a play-test prototype and a polished app — and it costs ~12 lines of SwiftUI to add. Apply broadly: arcade games, board games, card games, puzzles all benefit.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI): ZStack { Color(red: 0.12, green: 0.06, blue: 0.15).ignoresSafeArea(); RadialGradient(gradient: Gradient(colors: [Color.yellow.opacity(0.22), .clear]), center: UnitPoint(x: 0.2, y: 0.1), startRadius: 0, endRadius: 360); RadialGradient(...) }

RIGHT (HTML/CSS): background: radial-gradient(circle at 20% 10%, rgba(250,204,21,0.22), transparent 28rem), radial-gradient(circle at 80% 0%, rgba(34,211,238,0.16), transparent 26rem), linear-gradient(145deg, #170b1c 0%, #1f1026 48%, #35141d 100%);
WRONG: backgroundColor = .black, .ignoresSafeArea(), nothing else. Reads like a debug build.
Board game color picks: chess = warm beige + dark walnut accents; checkers = forest green felt + tan corner glow; poker = dark green felt + brass corner highlights.

guidelines.rule: Every clickable/interactive tile in a game (candy in match-3, card in solitaire, key on a piano keyboard, etc.) MUST have: (a) a vertical or 145deg linear-gradient fill, NOT a flat color. (b) An inner highlight - a rotated semi-transparent white blob or arc, inset ~12% from the edges, that gives the tile a 3D / glossy appearance. (c) A subtle 1.5px white-with-low-opacity stroke for definition. (d) A drop-shadow whose color is the tile's BOTTOM gradient color so the tile feels grounded. CSS: `.tile { background: linear-gradient(145deg, var(--tile-top), var(--tile-bottom)); border: 1.5px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.28); box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.4); position: relative }` plus an inner shine via `.tile::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: 12%; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.22); transform: rotate(18deg); border-radius: 40% }`.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The jewels look kind of lame, the graphics are very flat and boring.' Adding the gradient + inner shine + colored shadow makes a flat shape look like a candy-shop jewel without bundling any art assets. Same trick works for cards (gradient front + back), keys (white-to-cream gradient + shine), pieces (color-toned gradient + shadow).
examples: RIGHT (Match3 candy): see /Users/lawrencenorman/mystuff/src/mchatai_platform/mchatai-source/frameworks/macos-components/examples/match3/ContentView.swift (function candyTileBody) — gradient + inner shine + stroke + colored shadow
RIGHT (web Candy Match): see frameworks/web-components/examples/candy-match/candy-match.css — .tile + .tile::before + .tile.kind-N gradient classes
WRONG: RoundedRectangle.fill(Color.red) + Image(systemName: 'sparkle') with no gradient, no shine, no shadow — reads as a wireframe placeholder
Reskin guidance: when the user asks for a different theme (e.g. 'space-themed match-3'), keep the gradient+shine+stroke STRUCTURE, only swap the gradient color pairs.

guidelines.rule: Every game state transition that the player triggers MUST have a visible animation, not a snap-cut. Specifically: (1) Tile/piece moves: animate `transform: translate(...)` with `transition: transform 0.25s cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1)` (overshoot easing gives a spring feel). (2) Tile-swap (match-3, sliding puzzles): both tiles slide simultaneously toward each other via transform, then settle. (3) Tile/piece removal: scale to 1.3x + fade-out (`transition: transform 0.18s ease-out, opacity 0.18s ease-out`). (4) Refill / collapse (match-3, tetris): new tiles slide DOWN from above via a keyframe (`@keyframes drop { from { transform: translateY(-120%); opacity: 0 } to { transform: translateY(0); opacity: 1 } }`), or use the `effects.spawn-drop` Lego component. (5) Cascade chain: stagger by 0.20-0.25s per chain pass using `transition-delay` (or `animation-delay`) so the player can see each step. (6) Score increment: a brief scale-pulse on the score number via a keyframe, or use the `effects.score-rise` Lego component. NEVER use snap-cuts on user-initiated state changes - even 0.1s of motion is the difference between 'game' and 'spreadsheet'.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'the animations are still pretty lame, the jewels don't slide down, there is no pulsing hints, nothing.' Borrowing the web Candy Match's animation choreography (swap glide + pop+fade match + slide-from-top refill) made the game feel alive instead of teleporty. Apply broadly: chess piece moves should glide, card flips should rotate, hop-games should arc, everything you click should respond visibly.
examples: RIGHT (swap glide): withAnimation(.easeInOut(duration: 0.18)) { tileOffsets[a] = ...; tileOffsets[b] = ... }; DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.20) { engine.swap(a, b); tileOffsets[a] = .zero; tileOffsets[b] = .zero }
RIGHT (pop fade): withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.20)) { poppingTiles.formUnion(matched); ... } -> tile body uses .scaleEffect(isPopping ? 1.3 : 1.0).opacity(isPopping ? 0 : 1)
RIGHT (cascade stagger): for (i, event) in events.enumerated() { DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + Double(i) * 0.24) { ... } }
WRONG: engine.swap(a, b); engine.findMatches(); engine.collapse(); engine.refill() — all in one synchronous frame; the user sees the board jump from old state to new state with zero feedback.
Anti-pattern: rendering NEW tiles in their final position. They should slide IN from off-screen / above. Use transitions: .move(edge: .top).combined(with: .opacity).

guidelines.rule: After ~5 seconds of player idleness in a game with valid-move gameplay (match-3, sudoku, mahjong-tile-matching, etc.), the game MUST surface a hint by visibly pulsing one valid move. The hint should: (a) compute a real valid move via the engine's findPossibleSwap / findPossibleMove API, (b) animate the involved tiles with a soft scale (1.05x) + glow pulse on a 1s ease-in-out cycle, (c) cancel the hint as soon as the player makes any move. This is the difference between 'I'm stuck' and 'this game wants me to figure it out alone.' Cap idle threshold at 5-8s — any longer feels like punishment.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'no pulsing hints, nothing'. The web Candy Match doesn't have this either, but it's a UX standard for tile-swap puzzlers. Added to Match3 with a Timer.publish(every: 1) ticker + lastMoveAt tracking + findPossibleSwap helper. Same pattern works for Mahjong (highlight a matchable pair), Sudoku (highlight a cell with only one valid digit), Solitaire (highlight a moveable card).
examples: RIGHT (idle threshold + scale-pulse): @State var lastMoveAt = Date(); Timer.publish(every: 1) { if Date.now - lastMoveAt > 5 { hintPair = engine.findPossibleSwap(); withAnimation(...) { hintPulse.toggle() } } }
RIGHT (cancel on move): when the player commits any swap/move, set hintPair = nil; lastMoveAt = Date()
WRONG: showing the hint immediately after every move (turns it into a tutorial mode the player can't disable)
WRONG: never showing a hint (advanced players don't need it, but new players quit when they're stuck and don't realize valid moves exist)

guidelines.rule: Tile-based games (match-3, sliding puzzles, falling-block puzzle, Threes, Dominoes, Mahjong) MUST track each tile by a STABLE id, NOT by cell position. Give each tile { id: <unique>, ... } and render it as a positioned element keyed by that id (e.g. an absolutely-positioned .tile[data-id] whose transform: translate(x,y) updates with a CSS transition). When the same id moves to a new cell, the transition slides it smoothly. Re-rendering cells BY position (wiping and repainting grid[row][col]) defeats this: the browser sees 'old contents gone, new contents arrived' and snap-cuts. Users report it as 'animations are lacking' or 'tiles teleport' - a stable-identity miss, not an animation-tuning issue.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3 v2: 'the jewels don't slide down, the animation is just one image that shows up and goes away, its not real animation' — even though v2 had withAnimation + spring + transitions. Root cause: the View iterated `ForEach(engine.grid.allPoints(), id: \.self)` so cells were the identity, not tiles. When the engine collapsed a column, the same Match3Tile UUID landed at a new cell but SwiftUI couldn't tell because it tracked cells. v3 changed to `ForEach(tilesWithPositions(), id: \.tile.id)` and the slide animation worked correctly without any extra animation code. Universal architectural rule.
examples: RIGHT: `struct GameTile: Identifiable, Equatable { var id = UUID(); var symbol: String }` + `ForEach(engine.tilesWithPositions(), id: \.tile.id) { entry in tileView(entry.tile).position(positionFor(entry.point)) }` + `.animation(.spring(...), value: engine.grid)` — SwiftUI animates each tile's position transition.

RIGHT pattern for the model: when the engine collapses, PRESERVE existing tile structs (push them onto a stack, pop back into new positions) — only create new tile structs (with new UUIDs) when REFILLING empty cells from above.

WRONG: `ForEach(engine.grid.allPoints(), id: \.self) { point in tileView(at: point) }` — cells are the identity. When tile content changes at a cell, SwiftUI redraws in place; when cells reshuffle, no animation possible because there's no continuity.
WRONG: trying to fix bad animation by adding more `.animation()` modifiers, longer durations, or `.transition()`s — none of those help if SwiftUI can't tell which old element is the new element.

Real incident 2026-05-04: catalog Match3 v2 had every animation primitive correct (spring, withAnimation, transition: .move(edge: .top), staggered DispatchQueue) but felt teleporty. Switching to tile-id iteration (commit d4a15c1) fixed the slide-down without changing any animation code — SwiftUI did the work once it had stable identity.

Bonus: this is the SwiftUI equivalent of React's `key` prop. The principle is identical: stable identity → smooth transitions. The key insight to take to other tile-based games is that the MODEL needs the ID, not the View.

guidelines.rule: Falling tiles in tile-based games (match-3, falling-block puzzle, sliding-tile, match-three puzzle, Threes) MUST deform: stretch vertically (sx≈0.92, sy≈1.12) while falling, squash horizontally (sx≈1.18, sy≈0.86) for ~130ms when they land. This is principle #1 of the 12 Disney principles (squash-and-stretch) — the difference between 'graphic moves' and 'candy lands.' Tiles that scale uniformly or just translate feel weightless and stickered onto the board. The squash is volume-preserving (sx*sy≈1.0).
guidelines.why: Real match-three puzzle production animations validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 catalog reference rebuild (v6). Without squash-stretch the user said 'they feel like pngs that are just being animated.' With it the tiles read as having weight.
examples: RIGHT: phaseScales(.falling) returns (0.92, 1.12); phaseScales(.squashing) returns (1.18, 0.86); driven by AnimationIntensity.fallStretch and .squashAmount.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): use the `effects.animation-intensity` Lego component — one struct field controls both stretch and squash, presets handle game pace.
WRONG: tiles use a uniform scale (1.0,1.0) the entire fall; reads as weightless icons sliding.

guidelines.rule: Match clears (or any 'item disappears with feedback' moment) MUST bulge before they shrink — anticipation (12 principles #4) + follow-through (#5). Sequence: (a) scale up 1.20–1.30× over 60–100ms with easeOut, (b) shrink to 0 + fade with easeIn over 80–110ms. A flat fade (just opacity 1→0) feels mechanical; bulge-shrink feels satisfying. The eye registers 'something is about to happen' (bulge) BEFORE the disappearance (shrink). The whole motion takes ~180ms.
guidelines.why: v3/v4/v5 of the catalog Match3 used flat fades and the user reported 'animations are lacking, feels flat.' v6 with bulge-shrink read as 'satisfying pop.' Universal animation principle — applies to card flips, item pickups, balloon pops, any binary disappear event.
examples: RIGHT: withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.08)) { phase = .bulging }; sleep 80ms; withAnimation(.easeIn(duration: 0.10)) { phase = .shrinking }; commit clear after shrink.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): bulgeAmount=0.25, bulgeDuration=0.08, shrinkDuration=0.11 are the standard preset.
WRONG: withAnimation(.easeOut) { tile.opacity = 0; tile.scale = 0 }  — single-phase fade-shrink, no anticipation, feels mechanical.

guidelines.rule: Tile-based games MUST use DIFFERENT animation curves for different phases. Pop/bulge = a fast ease-out (CSS cubic-bezier(0.2, 0.8, 0.2, 1), ~80ms). Shrink/clear = ease-in (~100ms, accelerate as it disappears). Land/drop = an overshoot 'spring' feel (cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1), ~180ms). Idle restore = a gentle settle (~200ms ease-out). One global transition curve for everything reads as 'boring ease on everything' - different timings per phase is what separates 'alive' from 'sterile'.
guidelines.why: Real match-three puzzle uses different curves per phase (researched 2026-05-04). v3/v4/v5 of catalog Match3 used one global spring; user feedback was 'feels flat.' v6 with per-phase curves was the first one that 'felt like candy.' Universal — applies to any squash-stretch animated UI.
examples: RIGHT: animationFor(.bulging) returns .easeOut(0.08); animationFor(.shrinking) returns .easeIn(0.10); animationFor(.squashing) returns .spring(response:0.18, dampingFraction:0.45). Apply each via .animation(animationFor(phase), value: phase) on the tile.
WRONG: withAnimation(.spring) wrapping the entire match-resolve cycle — every phase rides one curve, feels uniform.

guidelines.rule: When applying a 'landing squash' after a column collapse, squash ONLY the tiles whose row actually changed (or brand-new tiles entering from above). Squashing every tile on the board creates the symptom 'the whole board is vibrating after every match' even if the match was in one corner. Snapshot pre-collapse positions (UUID → row), then after the collapse diff against new positions and squash only the tiles with row != preCollapseRow[id]. Tiles that didn't move stay perfectly still.
guidelines.why: Real bug 2026-05-04 in catalog Match3 v6.0–v6.2: squash applied to all 64 tiles after every cascade pass → user reported 'the whole thing still feels like it's vibrating' even after the neighbor-jiggle was fully removed. Fix in v6.3 was diff-by-UUID-row. Same trap applies to ANY game with a collapse + squash-on-land animation. Sister bug pattern: any 'apply effect to all tiles' after a localized event needs to be 'apply effect to only AFFECTED tiles.'
examples: RIGHT: var preCollapseRow: [UUID: Int] = [:]; for entry in tilesWithPositions() { preCollapseRow[entry.tile.id] = entry.point.row }; engine.collapseAndRefill(); let landed = tilesWithPositions().compactMap { entry in let id = entry.tile.id; if let oldRow = preCollapseRow[id] { return oldRow != entry.point.row ? id : nil } else { return id } }; for id in landed { phase[id] = .squashing }
WRONG: let landedTileIDs = tilesWithPositions().map { $0.tile.id }; for id in landedTileIDs { phase[id] = .squashing }  — squashes EVERY tile, reads as global board vibration.

guidelines.rule: Neighbor-jiggle (secondary motion = surrounding tiles wobble when a 4/5-match clears) should default to FALSE. Despite being the canonical 'secondary motion' (12 principles #6), in practice it almost ALWAYS reads as 'the whole board is vibrating' regardless of how tightly you scope radius/amplitude/phase. The bulge-shrink pop + slide-down + landing-squash already sell the explosion. Only enable neighbor-jiggle if you've validated it doesn't read as global shake on the user's actual hardware/render rate.
guidelines.why: Real attempt 2026-05-04 in catalog Match3: started at radius 2, amplitude 2.5–4.0, shared sin() clock. User: 'the whole set of candies vibrate.' Tightened to radius 1 orthogonal-only, amplitude 1.1–1.6, per-tile random phase offset. User STILL: 'all neighbors vibrating.' Removed entirely. Took multiple iterations to learn that the symptom isn't a tuning problem — it's that neighbor-jiggle is fundamentally hard to localize on a dense grid. AnimationIntensity defaults `enableNeighborJiggle = false` enforced this lesson.
examples: RIGHT: AnimationIntensity.standard.enableNeighborJiggle = false; rely on bulge-shrink + slide + landing-squash for impact feedback.
RIGHT (if you really want to try): radius 1 orthogonal only, amplitude ≤ 1.5, per-tile random phase, decay ≤ 200ms — and validate live with the user before committing.
WRONG: radius 2+ on dense grid → reads as global. Shared sin() clock for all jiggling tiles → phase-locked board shake.

guidelines.rule: Centralize the squash/bulge/spring magnitudes behind ONE intensity scale (a CSS custom property like --anim-intensity, or a single JS constant) instead of hardcoding magic numbers per effect. Pick a level by game pace: subtle for productivity-flavored puzzles (crossword/word/sudoku/utility), standard for mechanics-driven games (match-3/minesweeper/breakout), punchy for arcade-action (asteroids/pacman/kids-games). One value rescales every effect together; individual effects can override. Comment the choice (/* anim-intensity: subtle */) so it is scannable.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, tuning means hunting for magic numbers across the View; copying numbers from one game to another forgets to scale them for pace. AnimationIntensity centralizes the tuning surface. Filed 2026-05-04 after Match3 v6 work + user request 'might almost be nice to define controls that allow us to dial up and down some of this stuff so we can quickly tune a given game or app.'
examples: RIGHT: `private let intensity: AnimationIntensity = .standard  // AnimationIntensity: standard (match-3 = mechanics-driven feedback)` then use `intensity.bulgeAmount`, `intensity.flashScale`, etc. throughout the View.
RIGHT (override individual): `var i = AnimationIntensity.standard; i.flashScale = 0.5; i.flashOpacity = 0.4 // dial flashes back further` and assign to `intensity`.
WRONG: hardcoded `.scaleEffect(1.25)`, `.font(.system(size: 92))`, `.spring(response: 0.18, dampingFraction: 0.45)` scattered across the View — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Per-event flash overlays (sparkle for 3-match, burst for 4-match, lightning for 5-match — and any equivalent 'event happened here' visual) should be SCALED DOWN and SEMI-TRANSPARENT, not full-size solid graphics. Rule of thumb: glyph size ~60% of cell size, opacity 0.4–0.6, halo radius modest. Overly large/solid flash overlays read as 'sticker pasted on top of the gameplay' rather than 'effect emanating from the gameplay.' Real production overlays are accents, not centerpieces. Avoid giant text labels like '⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡' — the game-mechanic feedback (score increment, board change) is the centerpiece; the flash is punctuation.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on catalog Match3 v6.3: 'the animation with the star and the lightning bolt are a bit too big and should probably be a little more transparent.' v6.4 flashes use scale=0.6 + opacity=0.55 + halo=18, removed text labels — and the flashes still register but no longer dominate. AnimationIntensity captures this as `flashScale: 0.6, flashOpacity: 0.55` in the standard preset.
examples: RIGHT: Image(systemName: "sparkles").font(.system(size: 56 * intensity.flashScale, weight: .black)).foregroundStyle(color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity)).shadow(color: color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity * 0.8), radius: intensity.flashHaloRadius)
RIGHT: NO 'YOU DID IT' / '5-MATCH' text labels — the score increment + board change communicates the event.
WRONG: opaque 96pt bolt.fill + 150pt circle + 60pt halo + giant Text('⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡') label centered on top of the board. Reads as 'graphic asset' not 'effect.'

guidelines.rule: Game-flavor web mini-apps (arcade, match-3, card, puzzle, board games) SHOULD use the resources.audio-manager Lego component for sound instead of hand-rolling new Audio() / AudioContext plumbing. AudioManager provides pooled low-latency playback, a debounce guard, music fade in/out, a persisted mute (localStorage), and a semantic SFX dispatcher (audio.sfx('match'), 'hit', 'gameover', 'levelup', 'click'). It is iOS-Safari-safe (defers the first AudioContext.resume() to a user gesture). Pair with ui.mute-toggle, or apply the recipe.with-sound-effects addon. Do not double-wire: if the base game already has its own audio, stop and report 'already-sonified'.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after building SoundEngine component for catalog Match3 v7. User feedback: 'sound effects really add to the overall game experience.' Without a shared engine, every game reinvents pool management, debouncing, mute toggle, music fades — and gets 2-3 of them wrong (jarring volumes, no fade, plays on every render).
examples: RIGHT: import effects.sound-engine; @ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared; on match-clear: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2)
RIGHT (mute button): Button { sound.muted.toggle() } label: { Image(systemName: sound.muted ? "speaker.slash.fill" : "speaker.wave.2.fill") } — required by audio-always-mute-toggle rule.
WRONG: build AVAudioPlayer pool ad-hoc in ContentView; reinvent debouncing; forget mute persistence; skip music fades.

guidelines.rule: Any game with sound MUST expose a visible, persistent mute toggle in the always-visible UI (not hidden in a settings menu). Default state: unmuted at modest volume (intensity.standard.sfxVolume = 0.65). Mute state MUST persist across app restarts via UserDefaults. Use SF Symbols `speaker.wave.2.fill` (unmuted) / `speaker.slash.fill` (muted). Optional but recommended: bind to keyboard shortcut 'M'. SoundEngine.muted handles the persistence — just observe `sound.muted` for the icon swap.
guidelines.why: Universal UX rule. Users tab between games, take calls, share screens. Burying mute deep in a settings menu means the next time they alt-tab in, the SFX surprise everyone in the room. Filed 2026-05-04 alongside SoundEngine ship — incorporated as default behavior in the engine itself but the visible-button is a View-level requirement that can't be enforced by the Lego.
examples: RIGHT: speaker icon in the bottom controls bar, always visible. Tap → mute persists.
RIGHT: keyboardShortcut("m", modifiers: []) for power-users + .help() tooltip.
WRONG: mute hidden in a 'Preferences' menu / hamburger / kebab. Surfacing-cost too high.

guidelines.rule: For match-3 / cascade / chain-reaction games, pitch-shift the SFX up per cascade depth (depth 0 = neutral, depth 1 = +2 semitones, depth 2 = +4, etc.) for the satisfying ascending arpeggio match-three is famous for. With resources.audio-manager / Web Audio, raise pitch by setting the source playbackRate to 2 ** (semitones/12) (e.g. depth*2 semitones). Cap at depth 6 (+12 semitones = double pitch); beyond that it sounds chipmunky. Apply the same pattern to combo counters, enemy streak-kills, and multi-line-clear in falling-block puzzle.
guidelines.why: Real match-three puzzle production behavior validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 v7 implementation. Without pitch-shift, every cascade pop sounds identical → user can't tell they're chaining. With pitch-shift, the ear hears 'thing 1, thing 2, thing 3' as ascending tones and the cascade feels satisfying. Universal — applies to any game with chainable events.
examples: RIGHT: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2) — direct swap = neutral, first cascade = +2, second = +4, etc.
RIGHT (combos): sound.play(.arcadeExplosion, pitchSemitones: min(comboCount, 6) * 2)
WRONG: same flat tone on every cascade pop — user can't tell they're chaining.

guidelines.rule: Sound effects must fire on DISCRETE GAME EVENTS (match resolved, button tapped, enemy hit, level up), NOT inside a render/draw loop or reactive re-render. Web Audio resumes lazily on the first user gesture; debounce repeated SFX yourself (a ~30ms per-sound guard). If you call audio.sfx('x') from inside a requestAnimationFrame draw callback or a render that fires every frame, you hammer the engine and the SFX drift out of sync with what the user did. Place SFX calls inside the event handler where the thing happens (the click/keydown listener, or the engine step where matches resolve) - never the render.
guidelines.why: Same anti-pattern as 'wrote tile content into ForEach Text() body' — render-time side effects = unpredictable. Filed 2026-05-04 after wiring Match3: original instinct was to call sound.play(.match3Pop) inside the `flashView` ViewBuilder. That would have fired on every flash render, not just when the flash was created. Moved into the cascade-loop where events.kind is known once per resolution.
examples: RIGHT: in the cascade loop where match events resolve, exactly once per event: `for event in previewEvents { sound.play(matchSFX(for: event.kind), pitchSemitones: event.cascadeDepth * 2) }`
WRONG: `var body: some View { Text("\(score)").onAppear { sound.play(.scoreTick) } }` — fires every render.

WRONG: `.onChange(of: engine.score) { sound.play(.coinPickup) }` triggers on EVERY score change including the +30 from a 3-match → could fire 6+ times in a single cascade. Either debounce upstream or fire on the discrete match event, not the score derivative.

guidelines.rule: Same pattern as AnimationIntensity: pick a SoundIntensity preset by game pace. `.subtle` (sfxVol=0.35, musicVol=0.15) for productivity-flavored puzzles where audio is light punctuation. `.standard` (0.65/0.35) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper). `.punchy` (0.85/0.55) for arcade-action (asteroids, pacman, plants-vs-zombies, kids/casual). `.soft` (0.5/0.45) for relaxing/idle/ambient (solitaire, pattern matchers). Stamp the choice in a comment near the SoundEngine declaration: `// SoundIntensity: standard (match-3 mechanics-driven feedback)`.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, generators copy-paste a volume float and forget to scale for pace. Match-3 at .punchy is too loud; arcade at .subtle disappears. Presets capture the calibration. Mirrors the AnimationIntensity pattern intentionally so generators learn one mental model.
examples: RIGHT: SoundEngine.shared.intensity = .standard  // SoundIntensity: standard (match-3)
RIGHT (override): var i = SoundIntensity.standard; i.sfxVolume = 0.5; i.musicVolume = 0.25; SoundEngine.shared.intensity = i
WRONG: hardcoded `sound.play(.match3Pop, volume: 0.7)` everywhere — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Background music tracks MUST fade in (1.0–1.5s) and fade out (1.0s) — NEVER hard-cut. Hard cuts are jarring and feel like a glitch. SoundEngine.loop(_:fadeIn:) handles this: `sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5)`. Track switches: stopMusic(fadeOut: 0.3) THEN loop(.next, fadeIn: 1.0) — for a clean crossfade-via-stop. App-quit / sheet-dismiss / scene-change: stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) before transition. SFX are exempt — they're already short, fade is built into the sound itself.
guidelines.why: Universal audio production rule. Hard cuts on long-form audio = listener notices the seam = breaks immersion. Filed 2026-05-04 with SoundEngine ship — the engine enforces fade defaults so hard cuts only happen if a generator explicitly passes 0.
examples: RIGHT: sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5) on game start; sound.stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) on game-over screen.
WRONG: musicPlayer.play() / musicPlayer.stop() without fade. Listener hears the abrupt cut.

guidelines.rule: Progression-flavored games (match-3, arcade-with-waves, puzzles-with-difficulty-tiers, runners, idle clickers, platformers with stages) SHOULD use the web level Lego components instead of inventing target-score / progression math from scratch: entities.level-content-data (data-driven campaign + DifficultyScaler + localStorage save/load; LevelContentData.generate({ count, baseTarget, scale }) builds a smooth curve), entities.level-progression (runtime state + cumulative score for the hosted-page leaderboard bridge), and ui.level-select-screen (the picker). Read scaled gameplay knobs via level.params.<knob> instead of hardcoding per-level. Or apply the recipe.with-level-progression addon, which composes all three.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager ship. Without a shared component, every progression game writes ad-hoc 'targetScore *= 1.5' arithmetic, forgets to persist current level, and has no celebration moment — users finish level 1, see the same screen, and lose engagement. LevelManager + the celebration window turn 'beat target' into a reward.
examples: RIGHT: @StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "match3", baseTarget: 1500, baseMovesAllowed: 30, curve: .standard); HUD reads `Text("LEVEL \(levels.currentLevel)")` and `Text("\(levels.currentTarget)")`. On target hit: `levels.advance()`.
RIGHT (UI): scaleEffect on level pill = `1.0 + levels.celebrationProgress * 0.20`; level-up banner opacity = `Double(levels.celebrationProgress)`.
RIGHT (palette growth): `engine.symbols = Array(["A","B","C","D","E","F","G","H"].prefix(levels.suggestedPaletteSize))` — palette starts at 5 colors, grows by +1 every 5 levels, capped at 8.
WRONG: hardcoded `private let targetScore = 1500`, no progression. User beats it, sees nothing change, exits app, never returns.
WRONG: ad-hoc `if score >= 1500 { score = 0; targetScore = 2500 }` — no curve, no persistence, no celebration.

guidelines.rule: When a player advances a level, the UI MUST acknowledge it with a 1-3 second celebration: (a) a prominent 'LEVEL N' banner that inflates and fades, (b) a level-up SFX (SoundEngine.play(.levelUp) — pitch-shift +2 semitones for stronger effect), (c) a brief scale/glow pulse on the level pill / score readout, (d) a tactile pause (~0.5–1s) before the new board / wave is presented. Skipping the celebration means the player isn't told they accomplished something — and the dopamine hit of progression is the entire reason to play. LevelManager provides `celebratingLevelUp: Bool` and `celebrationProgress: CGFloat` (0→1→0 across the window) — bind animations to these.
guidelines.why: Universal game-design principle. Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Without celebration, a level-up feels like a level-down — the user hits the target and the game just changes the number. With celebration (banner + SFX + pulse), the user FEELS the win. Match3 v8 implementation: level pill scaleEffect(1.0 + celebrationProgress * 0.20), banner Text scaleEffect(0.7 + celebrationProgress * 0.6) + opacity(celebrationProgress), levelUp SFX with +2 pitch.
examples: RIGHT: levels.advance() triggers celebrationProgress ramp 0→1 over 0.5s, plateau 0.5s, 1→0 over 2s. View binds banner opacity/scale to celebrationProgress; SFX plays at the start.
RIGHT (audio sequence): on target hit → sound.play(.victory) → 450ms pause → sound.play(.levelUp, pitchSemitones: 2) → levels.advance() triggers banner.
WRONG: levels.advance() called silently with no UI reaction. Player isn't sure if anything happened.
WRONG: instant snap-cut to next level (no banner, no SFX, no delay). Feels like a glitch.

guidelines.rule: When a player fails a level (out of moves, lives depleted, time up), the game MUST allow replay of the SAME level — never auto-demote to a lower level or back to L1. Save the player's progression at all times. RESTART buttons should reset score/moves WITHIN the current level, not bump levels.currentLevel down. Players who hit a hard level will replay it many times; demoting them would be a punishment that destroys engagement. The only way to drop a level should be a deliberate user action (e.g. a 'Reset Progress' settings toggle, never the in-game RESTART button).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager. Hardcoded into LevelManager API: `replayCurrentLevel()` is a no-op on persistence; only `reset()` (explicit reset-progress action) resets currentLevel to 1. Universal UX rule — applied incorrectly, players quit the game forever after one rage-fail.
examples: RIGHT: RESTART button rebuilds engine, refreshes moves to levels.currentMovesAllowed, but does NOT touch levels.currentLevel.
RIGHT: 'Reset Progress' in settings menu calls levels.reset() — opt-in only.
WRONG: RESTART resets currentLevel to 1. Player who beat 5 levels and rage-quit on L6 has to redo all 5.

guidelines.rule: Higher levels should feel different across MULTIPLE dimensions, not just 'higher target number.' For tile/match games: grow the symbol palette (LevelManager.suggestedPaletteSize: 5→6 at L5, 7 at L10, 8 at L15), shrink moves budget if pacing demands it (.accelerating curve), bump animation intensity (LevelManager.suggestedAnimationIntensity: 'subtle' L1-4 → 'standard' L5-9 → 'punchy' L10+). For arcade games: spawn-rate (LevelManager.suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier: 1.0× → 3.0× capped at L20+), enemy variety, projectile speed, intermittent boss waves at L5/L10/L15. The TARGET getting larger is just one knob — alone, it produces 'same game, takes longer.' Multi-knob progression produces 'this level FEELS different.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Real match-three puzzle / match-three puzzle / etc. grow many things per level (board shape, special-tile generation rate, blockers, mission types). Reference apps in the catalog should at minimum grow palette + intensity for tile games, spawn-rate + intensity for arcade games. LevelManager exposes the suggested helpers so generators don't have to invent them.
examples: RIGHT (match-3): L1: 5 colors, .standard intensity. L5: 6 colors, .standard. L10: 7 colors, .punchy. L15: 8 colors, .punchy. Each level feels harder for a NEW reason.
RIGHT (arcade): L1: 2 enemies, slow projectiles, .standard intensity. L5: 4 enemies, faster projectiles, .standard. L10: 6 enemies + occasional bomb-spawn, .punchy.
WRONG: every level identical except target=basicTarget*1.4^N. Player perceives 'same game just slower to finish' — no surprise, no novelty, drops off.

guidelines.rule: Choose LevelCurve by intended play session length: `.casual` (1.5× per level, fast ramp) for short kid-friendly sessions where players should reach high levels in 10-15 minutes. `.standard` (1.4×) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper) where 30-60 min sessions are typical. `.hardcore` (1.3×, slow ramp) for endurance/idle/score-attack games designed for long-term engagement. `.accelerating` (gentle 1.3× early, climbs to 1.6× by L20+) for games that should onboard easily but become a real challenge — typical for puzzle franchises. `.custom` for games with non-monotonic difficulty (boss levels, breather levels, jumps).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Same generator-mental-model pattern as AnimationIntensity / SoundIntensity — pick a preset by game pace, not by inventing arithmetic. Wrong curve = engagement disaster: hardcore curve on a casual game = players quit at L4; casual curve on hardcore = players hit L99 in an hour and have nothing left.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "kid-game", curve: .casual) — fast progression, frequent level-up celebrations.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "match3", curve: .standard) — balanced.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "score-attack", curve: .hardcore) — slow ramp.
